params.text:
The hierarchy of control

COSHH requires employers to prevent exposure wherever reasonably practicable and, where prevention is not possible, to adequately control it. The hierarchy of control ranks measures from most to least effective, and you work down the list only when a higher option is not possible.

  • Eliminate – stop using the hazardous substance altogether, or avoid the task that creates it.
  • Substitute – swap it for a safer product, for example a milder cleaner or a ready-diluted solution.
  • Engineering controls – use physical measures such as ventilation, closed containers or dilution systems.
  • Safe systems of work – use good practice such as procedures, training, limiting how long a task takes and hand washing.
  • Personal protective equipment (PPE) – gloves, aprons, masks and eye protection, used as the last line of defence.

PPE is last because it only protects the individual wearing it, it can fail or be worn incorrectly, and it does nothing to remove the hazard itself.

params.taskDescription: Drag each word into the correct gap to complete these statements about the hierarchy of control.
params.textField: The most effective control is to *eliminate* the substance completely. If that is not possible, you might *substitute* it for something safer. Physical measures such as ventilation are known as *engineering* controls. Good procedures and training are called safe *systems* of work. Personal protective equipment is always the *last* line of defence because it only protects the person *wearing* it.
